nativeRange.habitat: Slow-moving or still freshwater streams, ditches, and flooded fields; among leaf litter, roots, and dense vegetation in lowland freshwater habitats

decorPreferences: small caves and terracotta tubes for territory and breeding
dense planting with Java moss, Anubias, and Cryptocoryne
smooth pebbles and leaf litter substrate
rocks and driftwood creating broken sight lines

diet.notes: The Freshwater Bumblebee Goby is a strict micro-carnivore that overwhelmingly prefers live or frozen small invertebrates. A reliable staple diet includes small bloodworms, mosquito larvae, micro-worms, Grindal worms, and brine shrimp nauplii; Tubifex can be offered in moderation. Dry foods such as micro-pellets or nano crumbles are typically ignored or accepted only reluctantly, and relying on dry food alone tends to result in slow decline. Some individuals can be trained to accept high-quality frozen bloodworm as a supplement, but a consistent supply of live or frozen invertebrates is essential for long-term health.

goodWith: small peaceful freshwater fish of similar size (microdevario, otocinclus)
dwarf freshwater shrimp in larger tanks with cover
other Brachygobius xanthomelas — best in same-species groups of 6+
ember tetras and similarly sized nano fish in spacious tanks
avoidWith: brackish-preferring Brachygobius congeners (B. doriae, B. xanthozonus, B. nunus) — differing salinity requirements cause husbandry conflict
larger fish that could eat or harass them
fast or boisterous fish that outcompete them at feeding
fish requiring soft acidic water incompatible with their harder neutral range

imageCandidates.notes: FishBase species photo gallery for Brachygobius xanthomelas (species ID 10447); reviewer should select best specimen photo from gallery page.
Wikimedia Commons category for B. xanthomelas; check for CC-licensed images of wild-type specimens showing characteristic yellow-and-black banding.

summary: The Freshwater Bumblebee Goby is a tiny bottom-dweller, reaching around 3 to 4.5 cm, with striking yellow-and-black banding. Unlike most trade-sold bumblebee gobies, Brachygobius xanthomelas is a true freshwater species and will decline in brackish water. Best kept in a nano setup with live or frozen micro-foods.
careNotes: The Freshwater Bumblebee Goby is one of the most commonly misidentified gobies in the hobby: the trade name 'Bumblebee Goby' is applied interchangeably to B. xanthomelas (a true freshwater species) and to brackish-preferring congeners such as B. doriae, B. xanthozonus, and B. nunus. The critical distinction for the keeper is water salinity — B. xanthomelas thrives in neutral to slightly alkaline fresh water (pH 7.0–8.0, gH 9–19) and will slowly decline in brackish conditions that suit its congeners. When purchasing from stores where species are mixed, assume the fish may be misidentified and establish care based on species confirmation or a cautious freshwater default. A dedicated species tank or nano community with similarly sized peaceful fish works best; males are territorial toward each other and require hiding places and broken sight lines to manage aggression. Minimum recommended group is 6 to distribute male territorial disputes. Feeding is the primary challenge — this species is a strict micro-carnivore that overwhelmingly prefers live or frozen small invertebrates (bloodworm, mosquito larvae, micro-worms, Grindal worms, brine shrimp nauplii). Most individuals will not accept dry food reliably; a steady supply of live or frozen micro-foods is essential for long-term health. See also: fw-fish-291 (brackish Bumblebee Goby) — the brackish-preferring trade counterpart often sold under the same name.
breedingNotes: Breeding is achievable in a well-established species tank but requires specific conditions. Males establish territory around a small cave or hollow tube and court females with lateral displays. Spawning occurs inside the cave, where the male deposits and guards a clutch of adhesive eggs. The male fans and guards the eggs until hatching in approximately 5–7 days at 26°C. Fry are tiny and require infusoria or paramecia initially, progressing to newly hatched brine shrimp nauplii and micro-worms within 10–14 days. Separating fry from adults improves survival rates significantly. Conditioning pairs with live foods prior to breeding attempts substantially improves spawning frequency.

primary.name: SeriouslyFish — Brachygobius xanthomelas
primary.notes: Primary source. Confirmed B. xanthomelas as a true freshwater species distinct from brackish congeners B. doriae, B. xanthozonus, and B. nunus. Provided water parameters (temperature 24–28°C, pH 7.0–8.0, hardness 9–19 dH), adult size (to 4.5 cm), habitat (lowland freshwater streams in SE Asia), diet (live/frozen invertebrates), and breeding behaviour (cave spawner, paternal egg guard).

additional.name: FishBase — Brachygobius xanthomelas
additional.notes: Cross-checked taxonomy (Gobiidae, Gobiiformes), native range (Indonesia — Borneo and Sumatra; also recorded from Thailand and Malaysia), adult size (max TL ~4.5 cm), and freshwater habitat classification. Confirmed species-level distinction from other Brachygobius.

additional.name: Aquarium Co-Op — Bumblebee Goby Care Guide
additional.notes: Trade-oriented care guide confirming feeding challenge (live/frozen preferred, dry rarely accepted), minimum tank size (~40 L for a small group), need for hiding spots and caves, and intermediate care rating. Notes the common misidentification between freshwater B. xanthomelas and brackish congeners in store stock.

additional.name: Practical Fishkeeping — Bumblebee Gobies
additional.notes: UK hobby publication article confirming the identification problem in retail stores (multiple Brachygobius species sold under same common name), freshwater vs. brackish distinction, diet requirements (live invertebrates as staple), territorial behaviour among males, and breeding overview (cave spawner, male guards eggs ~5–7 days to hatching).

additional.name: Seriously Fish — Brachygobius genus overview
additional.notes: Genus-level context confirming that B. xanthomelas is the primary freshwater-tolerant species in the genus and detailing how it differs from brackish-preferring congeners in salinity tolerance and habitat preference.

fish.breedingNotes: The Freshwater Bumblebee Goby is a cave spawner. The male establishes territory around a cave or tube, guards an adhesive egg clutch through hatching, and is best conditioned beforehand with live foods. Fry are extremely small and require infusoria or paramecia initially, progressing to newly hatched brine shrimp nauplii within the first two weeks. Breeding is achievable in a dedicated setup but is not routine.
